prompt:
Render-farm tooling ticket. Our job-card runner logs every command it executes as an argv array, and operators paste one-line commands into job cards that we have to split back into argv. Today both directions go through a pile of regexes that fall over on filenames with spaces, on O'Brien, and on empty arguments — last week a card with 'Monthly Report $Q3.txt' silently became three arguments and the wrong file got archived. I want a small module, ShellLine in shell_line.rb, that owns this for good, with OUR rules pinned by tests (test_shell_line.rb, run with `ruby test_shell_line.rb` — that file is the contract, don't edit it). And write the rules out yourself: the stdlib helper doesn't match our error handling and the first test checks you didn't just load it.

ShellLine.split(line) -> array of words, POSIX-shell style: unquoted spaces, tabs and newlines separate words and runs of them collapse; text in single quotes is literal down to the last byte (no escapes inside, newlines kept); text in double quotes groups too, but there a backslash escapes only " \ $ and backtick — before any other character the backslash itself is kept; outside quotes a backslash makes the next character literal, whatever it is; a backslash directly before a newline (outside single quotes) is a line continuation and both characters disappear; adjacent quoted/unquoted pieces glue into one word, and '' or "" alone is a real empty word. A line that ends inside a quote or right after a bare backslash raises ArgumentError — job cards are one line, there is no 'keep typing' prompt.

ShellLine.escape(word) -> a single word rendered safely: empty becomes ''; words made only of letters, digits and @ % + = : , . / _ - pass through untouched; anything else is wrapped in single quotes with each embedded single quote rendered as the close-escape-reopen sequence '\'' (quote, backslash-quote, quote).

ShellLine.join(argv) -> each word escaped, joined with single spaces.

The law that matters most: ShellLine.split(ShellLine.join(argv)) == argv for every argv, including empty strings, quotes, backslashes and newlines inside arguments — there's a table of nasty ones in the tests.
